findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the per-file LOC attribute on `AnalyzerResult.loc` (e.g., `loc.files: list[tuple[str, int]]` or `loc.per_file: dict[str, int]`) that `over_cap_count` will iterate — either cite the specific sub-field from 3b30's/18f9's final schema or add it to those tickets' ACs before implementing this ticket.
evidence: Plan: 'compute from AnalyzerResult [rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc].loc'
Ticket 3b30 AC: 'AnalyzerResult (fields loc/complexity/duplication, each optional=None when unsupported)' — no sub-field names for loc
Ticket 18f9: 'AnalyzerResult.loc (per-file LOC + max)' and '.loc.max_loc' — only max_loc named, per-file attribute unnamed
AC test asserts: 'files at 100 and 850, cap 800 -> over_cap_count==1' — requires iterating per-file sizes, not just max_loc
No grep match for 'AnalyzerResult' or 'size_cap' in src/rebar (these are prerequisite-created)
findings.finding: The plan asserts it will compute `over_cap_count` by iterating per-file LOC from `AnalyzerResult.loc`, but no prerequisite ticket specifies the attribute name(s) for the per-file collection within `AnalyzerResult.loc`. Ticket 3b30 defines `loc` as a field (optional=None when unsupported) without naming its sub-structure; ticket 18f9 says it carries 'per-file LOC + max' but only names `max_loc` explicitly (in `.loc.max_loc`). The plan's AC test (`test_configured_positive`) requires iterating individual file sizes (100, 850) against a cap — a computation that requires a per-file attribute name that is never cited.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) — `module_size_distribution`/`oversized_module_count` bullet; AC line: `test_configured_positive`
scenarios: Implementer writes `for file_loc in result.loc.files` but 3b30 names it `result.loc.per_file` or `result.loc.entries` — silent AttributeError at runtime
Test test_configured_positive is written against the wrong attribute shape, passes with mocking but fails against the real AnalyzerResult

The existing _spec() adapter only pulls repo_root/since/until from ctx (git_metrics.py:245–252). An implementer must invent attribute names, risking mismatch with whatever the prerequisite ticket (e7a0) actually puts on the context.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) section; git_metrics.py _spec() adapter lines 242–252
scenarios: Implementer reads ctx.size_cap but prerequisite puts it at ctx.code_health_config.size_cap — compute always sees None, always returns Unavailable even in the configured case
AC2 test passes with synthetic ctx built to match one naming assumption while the real CLI context uses a different attribute path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `oversized_module_count` seed in `registry.py` must be removed (or replaced in-place) before the new git_metrics.py compute can register — the idempotent `register()` guard will silently no-op a duplicate id.
evidence: 'oversized_module_count: … this ticket gives it a real compute'
registry.py: REGISTRY already contains MetricSpec(id='oversized_module_count', compute=_no_data_yet)
git_metrics.py register(): `if spec.id not in existing: REGISTRY.append(spec)` — skips ids already registered
findings.finding: The plan says 'oversized_module_count is today a seed stub (_no_data_yet); this ticket gives it a real compute' but does not specify the mechanism for replacing that seed. The current register() in git_metrics.py skips any id already present in REGISTRY (idempotent-on-id). Because registry.py already seeds 'oversized_module_count' with _no_data_yet, a naïve new _spec(..., 'oversized_module_count', ...) added to register() will be silently dropped — the stub will persist and AC2 will fail.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) section; registry.py lines 142–149; git_metrics.py register() lines 264–277
scenarios: Implementer adds oversized_module_count to git_metrics.register(); it is silently no-op'd; the seed _no_data_yet remains; test_configured_positive fails with Unavailable instead of a real value
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `oversized_module_count` MetricSpec from the REGISTRY seed list in registry.py and add it to git_metrics.register() alongside module_size_distribution.

The concrete shape of `.loc` (e.g., `dict[str, int]` keyed by file path, a dataclass field, a list of per-file structs) is never specified in this plan. The AC test pins `over_cap_count==1, max_loc==850` without stating how the reader traverses `.loc` to derive those values — an unverified capability assertion that the implementer cannot resolve until 3b30 merges.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) / AC line: 'Configured positive path'
scenarios: Implementer writes `max(result.loc.values())` but `.loc` is a dataclass with a `.per_file` list — test fails at runtime only after 3b30 merges.
Implementer assumes `.loc` is a `dict[path, int]` but 18f9 builds it as `AnalyzerResult.loc.max_loc` (a scalar), causing the distribution logic to be wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Per-metric post-migration semantics' section: e.g., 'AnalyzerResult.loc is a `dict[str, int]` mapping module path to LOC count; `max_loc = max(loc.values())` and `over_cap_count = sum(1 for v in loc.values() if v > size_cap)`.' If 3b30 uses a different shape, sync this plan with 3b30's AC before implementation.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify how AnalyzerResult reaches the compute callback. The existing _spec adapter reads only repo_root/since/until from ctx; the new compute must either (a) call resolve_analyzer(lang)(ctx.repo_root) inline, (b) read a pre-computed AnalyzerResult from ctx, or (c) receive the analyzer via ctx. These options have different ordering constraints (who runs the analyzer, when, and with which error-handling contract), and the plan text leaves the implementer to invent this junction without guidance.
findings.location: Mechanism section — module_size_distribution / oversized_module_count; Plan corrections (round 4) SIZE CAP SOURCE
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Mechanism section: 'The compute callable for module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count calls resolve_analyzer("python") to obtain the Analyzer, then invokes analyzer.analyze(ctx.repo_root) inline; if resolve_analyzer returns None or analyze() raises/returns Unavailable, the compute returns None (yielding Unavailable from registry.evaluate()). This keeps compute callbacks self-contained, consistent with the existing _spec adapter pattern.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the 'source' provenance label for the new module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count MetricSpec entries. The existing registry.py seed stubs for these use source='structural'; the git_metrics.py registered specs use source='git'; the new compute is backed by an scc CLI (a structural tool, not git). Leaving this unresolved risks the implementer inheriting the wrong label (e.g. keeping source='git' from the _spec helper, or keeping source='structural' from the seed stub), which affects is_authoritative() correctness and downstream signal segregation.
findings.location: Mechanism section — module_size_distribution / oversized_module_count; Files: src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py, src/rebar/metrics/registry.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism by which AnalyzerResult reaches the compute callback — e.g., 'ctx carries an analyzer_result: AnalyzerResult | None attribute populated by the metrics CLI before dispatching' — so the _spec adapter can be written unambiguously.

findings.finding: No existing mechanism delivers an AnalyzerResult to a MetricSpec compute callback. The current _spec adapter in git_metrics.py reads only repo_root/since/until from ctx (lines 245–251); no AnalyzerResult field exists on any context object in the codebase. The plan states readers 'compute from AnalyzerResult.loc' but does not specify how AnalyzerResult is obtained inside the compute lambda — whether by calling a resolver, reading a ctx attribute, or something else.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6); Files likely touched: src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py
scenarios: Implementer must decide between (a) calling a resolver function inside compute, (b) reading ctx.analyzer_result, or (c) some other pattern — all are equally valid guesses from the plan text, leading to divergent implementations.

If ctx does not carry an analyzer_result attribute, the callback must call an external function that performs analysis (introducing I/O inside a compute callback, which breaks determinism expectations).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'The metrics context object will expose `ctx.analyzer_result: AnalyzerResult | None`; when None (unconfigured or non-Python client), the compute callback returns None → Unavailable. The CLI populates this attribute by calling `rebar.metrics.analyzer.analyze(ctx.repo_root)` before dispatch.'

Dead computation code for a deregistered metric is an incomplete migration.

scenarios: Implementer removes the registration and renames/abstracts the path strings but leaves `_read_cap` and the cap_change_events compute block in place as dead code, satisfying all ACs while leaving the file inconsistent with the stated goal of dropping the hardcoded logic.

findings.finding: `oversized_module_count` is currently a seed stub in `registry.py` with `source='structural'`. The plan gives it a real compute from `AnalyzerResult.loc`, but never specifies whether this spec stays in `registry.py` (in-place update) or is moved into / re-registered by `git_metrics.py`'s `register()`. If it remains in `registry.py` with `source='structural'` while the compute now reads from the git/analyzer path, the provenance label will be incorrect. The idempotent guard in `register()` would also silently block a second registration attempt.
findings.location: Mechanism section; src/rebar/metrics/registry.py:143-149; Files section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `cap_change_events` deregistration against the parent epic (839b): either update 839b to explicitly authorise removing the metric from the registry (which stales 839b's plan-review and requires re-review), or revise the leaf to re-express the metric via configurable path instead of deleting it.
evidence: Leaf: 'cap_change_events: tracks edits to .github/module-size-limit.txt — a rebar-governance artifact with no client analogue. DEREGISTER the spec from the portable registry (remove from register())'
Parent epic 839b AC: 'No hardcoded src/rebar / .github/module-size-limit.txt; scan root + size policy are per-project config.'
Parent epic does not say 'deregister' or 'remove from registry'; it says make it config-driven.
findings.finding: The leaf permanently deregisters `cap_change_events` from the portable registry (removes it from `register()`), but the parent epic's AC says 'No hardcoded src/rebar / .github/module-size-limit.txt; scan root + size policy are per-project config' — implying re-expression via config, not outright deletion of the metric. The leaf redefines the parent's 'make it config-driven' deliverable as 'drop it entirely', which is a scope divergence the parent does not authorise.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics / cap_change_events bullet; parent epic 839b AC 'No hardcoded … scan root + size policy are per-project config'
scenarios: Implementor deletes cap_change_events from the registry; but the parent epic's intent was to re-express it via configurable scan root — a client that does have a cap-change governance file now loses the metric entirely rather than getting it via config.
The parent epic's plan-review attestation was signed against a scope that does not include deregistration; the leaf silently widens its own authority.

git_metrics.py:77: `return int((Path(repo_root) / _LIMIT_FILE).read_text(...))` — raises FileNotFoundError if absent
registry.py evaluate(): calls spec.compute(context) with no try/except — any exception propagates to caller
findings.finding: The existing module_size_distribution raises FileNotFoundError when _read_cap() cannot read the limit file (git_metrics.py:77). The plan's AC5 expects the unconfigured path to return honest Unavailable (None from compute) — but the plan does not specify how the new compute handles missing/None AnalyzerResult or missing size_cap on ctx without raising an exception that would propagate out of evaluate() uncaught.
findings.location: AC bullet 5 (Unconfigured analyzer -> Unavailable); git_metrics.py _read_cap() line 77; registry.py evaluate() lines 103–122
scenarios: Foreign repo has no AnalyzerResult on ctx; compute tries to access ctx.analyzer.loc; AttributeError propagates through evaluate(); CLI crashes instead of returning Unavailable
size_cap is None on ctx; compute passes None to arithmetic; TypeError raised and leaks errno
findings.suggested_fix: In the compute closure: `if not (analyzer := getattr(ctx, 'analyzer', None)) or not (size_cap := getattr(ctx, 'size_cap', None)): return None` before accessing loc data.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision rule co-located with the G6 compute description specifying exactly which condition(s) on ctx trigger the Unavailable path (e.g. 'if getattr(ctx, "size_cap", None) is None or getattr(ctx, "analyzer_result", None) is None: return None') and which exceptions must be caught to prevent errno leakage.
evidence: Plan AC: 'Unconfigured analyzer -> Unavailable, NOT {count:0,max_loc:0} authoritative and NO leaked errno'
git_metrics.py:242-252: compute() returns None only when ctx is None; no other guard
No specification of what constitutes 'unconfigured' in a non-None ctx (missing size_cap? missing analyzer attribute? FileNotFoundError from analyzer?)
T8(b): processing/decision rule referenced but not co-located with the adapter schema that needs it; T8(d): fallback for a failed sub-step (missing config/analyzer) is not specified
findings.finding: The 'unconfigured analyzer → Unavailable' decision rule is stated in AC bullet 5 but is not co-located with — or derivable from — the new compute adapter specification. The existing _spec adapter (git_metrics.py:242-252) only returns None when ctx is None; it has no protocol for a non-None ctx that lacks an analyzer or size_cap. The plan names the observable result (Unavailable, no errno) without specifying the guard clause that produces it.
findings.location: AC bullet 5: 'Unconfigured analyzer -> Unavailable, NOT {count:0,max_loc:0} authoritative and NO leaked errno. PROVE: pytest … test_foreign_repo_honest_unavailable'
scenarios: A non-Python client passes a valid ctx but without an analyzer attribute — the current _spec adapter calls module_size_distribution(repo_root) which hits the filesystem, reads the old limit file, and returns fabricated zeros rather than Unavailable
Implementer catches FileNotFoundError but not AttributeError — errno leaks on a different missing-attribute path

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

citation [rebar:e7a0-acef-ec67-42bb] ('`.loc plus `size_cap`/`size_near_fraction`, which come from the `[code_health]` ') names no VERIFIED upstream prerequisite: P neither declares `depends_on -> e7a0-acef-ec67-42bb` nor is blocked by it. Add a `depends_on` edge to e7a0-acef-ec67-42bb, ensure e7a0-acef-ec67-42bb `blocks` this ticket, or remove the citation. Until then the cited symbol is grounded as normal (fails closed).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `cap_change_events` deregistration (e.g., a revert commit reference, a grace-period deprecation stub, or confirmation that no external consumers exist based on a registry query).
evidence: 'DEREGISTER the spec from the portable registry (remove from register()), documented in the close note'
No section describes how to reverse this if a downstream consumer is broken by the removal.
T4 criterion: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'
findings.finding: `cap_change_events` is being permanently removed from the metric registry (a breaking, irreversible change for any consumer that lists or queries this metric), but the plan provides no rollback path, back-out procedure, or expand-contract sequencing — only an acknowledgement and a close note.
findings.location: Per-metric semantics section / AC3
scenarios: A downstream consumer (e.g., a dashboard or alert rule) that references cap_change_events by name breaks silently after deploy; without a rollback plan the team must hand-patch the consumer or revert the commit manually with no documented procedure.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) confirm via a registry/grep audit that no external consumers reference cap_change_events and document that audit result in the plan, making the removal demonstrably safe; or (b) add a one-ticket deprecation stub that returns Unavailable before full removal so consumers have a grace period.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale explaining why consuming AnalyzerResult.loc via ctx (rather than, e.g., injecting only cap + scan-root as plain ctx attributes) is the right seam — e.g. 'collocates all code-health data in one pre-computed result, avoids a second filesystem walk, and lets non-Python repos return honest Unavailable without special-casing'.
evidence: ABSENCE: the plan explains 'what' changes (consume AnalyzerResult.loc + size_cap/size_near_fraction from ctx) but never explains 'why' this seam was chosen over alternatives
No 'Rationale' or 'Why this approach' section exists in the plan
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing AnalyzerResult-on-ctx as the architectural seam over simpler alternatives (e.g. injecting only the cap value and scan-root path via ctx, keeping the fs-walk local to git_metrics.py). A positive rationale is required by G6.
findings.location: Problem section and Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) section
scenarios: An implementer unsure why AnalyzerResult is on ctx might choose a different integration point (e.g. calling the analyzer inline inside compute()) and introduce a correctness or performance issue

findings.finding: The 5th AC bullet's PROVE command still references the OLD file paths (`tests/unit/test_git_metrics.py tests/unit/test_git_metrics_heldout.py`) that Round 4 explicitly corrected to the `tests/unit/metrics/` subdirectory. An executor following the main AC section will run pytest against nonexistent files (or wrong files if they happen to exist), silently voiding the proof.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 5th bullet: 'git-delta + agent_process metrics unchanged; updated git_metrics tests green. PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/test_git_metrics.py tests/unit/test_git_metrics_heldout.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 5th AC bullet's PROVE line with: `PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py`

findings.finding: AC bullet 5's PROVE command still references the OLD test paths (`tests/unit/test_git_metrics.py tests/unit/test_git_metrics_heldout.py`) that Round 4 explicitly corrected to `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py` and `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py`. The PROVE string was not updated when the Files section was corrected.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — bullet 5 PROVE command

findings.finding: `tests/unit/metrics/test_module_size_trend.py` remains listed in the Files section as '(new)' and in AC bullet 4's PROVE command, but Round 4 withdraws the real trend implementation and relocates the trend test to `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py::test_trend_unavailable`. The stale file reference makes the Files section and original AC bullet 4 contradictory.
findings.location: Files section; Acceptance criteria bullet 4; Plan corrections (round 4)

findings.finding: The Files section lists `tests/unit/metrics/test_module_size_trend.py (new)` as a file to create, but Plan corrections route the only remaining trend test into `tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py::test_trend_unavailable`, making `test_module_size_trend.py` an orphan with no described content.
findings.location: Files section ('tests/unit/metrics/test_module_size_trend.py (new)') vs. Plan corrections ('PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py::test_trend_unavailable')
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `tests/unit/metrics/test_module_size_trend.py (new)` from the Files section since the only surviving trend AC points to `test_git_metrics.py::test_trend_unavailable`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `test_configured_positive` to assert the structure/content of `module_size_distribution` (e.g. that it contains entries for both the 100-LOC and 850-LOC synthetic modules, keyed/shaped per the metric contract), not only the aggregate `over_cap_count` and `max_loc` values.
evidence: AC #2 states: 'files at 100 and 850, cap 800 -> over_cap_count==1, max_loc==850' — only counts are asserted
Per-metric semantics: 'module_size_distribution … compute from AnalyzerResult.loc' — the distribution's structure is unspecified in the AC
No AC asserts the keys, schema, or content of the module_size_distribution dict
findings.finding: The acceptance criterion for `test_configured_positive` only verifies aggregate outputs (`over_cap_count==1, max_loc==850`) and does not assert the structure or content of the `module_size_distribution` return value itself. Since `module_size_distribution` is a newly-wired metric reading from AnalyzerResult LOC (replacing the old scan), its return shape (keys, bucket labels, per-file entries, or whatever the dict format is) is unvalidated — a malformed distribution dict would pass the AC while delivering a broken user-facing metric.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC #2: `test_configured_positive`
scenarios: module_size_distribution returns an empty dict, wrong keys, or inverted values — test passes, metric is silently broken for consumers
Distribution includes wrong file entries or loses per-module granularity after migration — undetected by the AC
findings.suggested_fix: In the test fixture with files at LOC 100 and 850, assert that `module_size_distribution` is a dict/list whose entries include both modules (e.g. `{file_a: 100, file_b: 850}` or equivalent schema), validating that the per-module data survived the migration from the hardcoded scan to AnalyzerResult.loc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `oversized_module_count`'s compute delegates to `module_size_distribution` (extracting `over_cap_count`) or re-scans independently; if the latter, justify why the existing `module_size_distribution` result cannot be reused.
evidence: git_metrics.py:112 already returns `'over_cap_count': over_cap_count` inside `module_size_distribution`'s result dict
registry.py:143 seeds `oversized_module_count` as a separate MetricSpec with `_no_data_yet`

Plan states: 'oversized_module_count is today a seed stub (_no_data_yet); this ticket gives it a real compute' — without specifying whether it delegates to module_size_distribution or re-derives independently
findings.finding: `oversized_module_count` is planned to receive a 'real compute' as a separate MetricSpec, but `module_size_distribution` already computes and returns `over_cap_count` in its result dict (git_metrics.py:112). The plan is silent on whether the new `oversized_module_count` compute delegates to `module_size_distribution` (extracting the key) or independently re-scans/re-derives the same value. An independent re-scan would be NIH against its sibling within the same file.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics / `oversized_module_count` bullet; src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py:112; src/rebar/metrics/registry.py:143
scenarios: Implementer writes a standalone re-scan loop for `oversized_module_count`, duplicating the LOC iteration already in `module_size_distribution`, causing two full directory scans per evaluation cycle and divergence risk if one is updated without the other.
findings.suggested_fix: Have `oversized_module_count`'s compute call `module_size_distribution(...)` and return `result['over_cap_count']`, eliminating any duplication. The plan should state this explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to AGENTS.md (or an equivalent design doc) recording the decision that portable metric readers must consume AnalyzerResult via ctx rather than scanning the filesystem, so future contributors have a persistent reference.
evidence: 'compute from AnalyzerResult [rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc].loc plus size_cap/size_near_fraction, which come from the [code_health] config section … resolved onto the context object (read from ctx, no hardcoded file)'
No mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
Plan crosses metrics layer → analyzer layer boundary for the first time (previously readers scanned filesystem directly).
findings.finding: The plan establishes a new architectural pattern — metric readers consume AnalyzerResult via the context object rather than scanning the filesystem directly — but does not call for updating AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design doc to record this decision, leaving future ticket authors without a persistent reference to follow.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics / Files likely touched
scenarios: A future ticket author adds a new metric reader, doesn't know the 'consume AnalyzerResult via ctx' contract, and reverts to hardcoded scanning — silently re-introducing the same problem this ticket fixes.
A reviewer of a future ticket can't point to a persistent reference explaining why direct filesystem scanning is forbidden for portable metric readers.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to AGENTS.md under a 'Metric Reader Contract' or 'Architecture Decisions' heading: 'Metric readers MUST consume AnalyzerResult via the context object (ctx) and read thresholds from the [code_health] config section. Direct filesystem scanning (e.g., src/rebar/**/*.py, .github/*.txt) is prohibited in portable readers — it produces fabricated zeros for non-Python clients.' Reference the rebar ticket IDs for traceability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `test_git_metrics_heldout.py::test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels` as a specific update target: the `named` set on line 98 must have `cap_change_events` removed after deregistration.
evidence: Line 98: named = {"module_size_distribution", "churn", "cap_change_events", "refactor_to_addition_ratio"}
Plan says heldout 'MUST be updated' but names no specific function to update
The assertion `named = {..., 'cap_change_events', ...}` will fail after deregistration since REGISTRY will no longer contain a spec with that id and source='git'
findings.finding: test_git_metrics_heldout.py::test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels (line 98) explicitly asserts that cap_change_events IS a member of the set of registered git metrics. After this ticket removes it from register(), this assertion fails — but the plan names the heldout file as 'MUST be updated' without enumerating this specific test function or its assertion, making it easy to miss.
findings.location: tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py:92-103 / Grounding section: 'Existing tests that pin the OLD behavior live at … test_git_metrics_heldout.py … MUST be updated'
scenarios: Developer updates the heldout file for the new tests (test_configured_positive etc.) but misses test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels, leaving a latent red test that was not called out in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Grounding or Files-likely-touched section, add: 'In test_git_metrics_heldout.py, update test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels to remove cap_change_events from the named set; update test_cap_change_events to reflect that the function still exists but is no longer registered.'

evidence: Parent epic 839b AC: 'Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract.'
Leaf plan: no docs file in 'Files likely touched', no docs-related AC, no mention of user-guide or reuse-surface.
Sibling stories 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0, e7a0, 3b30: none include a docs deliverable in their file_impact or ACs.
findings.finding: The parent epic (839b) has an explicit AC requiring documentation: 'Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract.' No sibling story (18f9, 21fb, 3ba0, e7a0, 3b30) claims this deliverable, and the leaf has zero docs surface. The leaf's scope is silent on docs, implying the epic AC is being dropped with no ticket owning it.
findings.location: Files likely touched / Acceptance criteria sections of the leaf; parent epic 839b AC (docs bullet)
scenarios: Epic ships with all implementation stories complete but the docs AC is never delivered because no leaf owns it — the parent's acceptance criterion is silently skipped.
